response:
**Results**

We evaluated ten experimental variants of our proposed method on the validation set, measuring validation accuracy as the primary metric. Each variant was independently run with five random seeds; we report mean accuracy and standard deviation across seeds for each configuration (Table 1).

The baseline configuration, which omits the proposed component, achieved a validation accuracy of 67.8±0.6%. Of the nine variants incorporating the proposed component, only variant_08 surpassed the baseline, reaching 70.4±0.5%, representing an absolute improvement of +2.6 percentage points over the baseline. Notably, this gain is accompanied by reduced variability across seeds, suggesting increased robustness for this configuration.

The remaining eight variants underperformed relative to the baseline. Their accuracies were as follows: variant_01 (67.2±0.8%), variant_02 (66.9±0.7%), variant_04 (65.1±0.9%), variant_05 (66.3±1.0%), variant_06 (64.7±0.7%), variant_07 (65.8±0.8%), variant_09 (66.5±0.7%), and variant_10 (65.9±0.8%). All of these variants exhibited lower mean accuracy and, in some cases, increased variance compared to the baseline.

These results indicate that the effectiveness of the proposed component is highly sensitive to its configuration. While most variants yielded no improvement or even degraded performance, the specific configuration in variant_08 led to a substantial and consistent benefit. This highlights the importance of careful tuning and further analysis to understand which aspects of variant_08 drive the observed performance gains. Overall, our findings demonstrate the potential of the proposed approach, provided that appropriate hyperparameters or design choices are made.
